Abbotts En Route to U. S. A., Honolulu, Hawaiian Islands, May 14. Editor Robert S. Abbott and wife Wendy, with their three weeks on a visit, left the part this week for the States. While here they were royally entertained by Mr. Abbott, and the natives joined in making their stay in Honolulu a pleasant place to plaster it after spending fourteen years of hard work making the Defender the "World's Greatest Weekly Newspaper"; and the natives spirits. They will arrive in Chicago on or about May 20. Word comes that Mr. Abbott has been put on a tour of the farming and the new Hawaiian national convention in June. His more important duty after his arrives will be the furnishing of the new Hawaiian national convention to be ready the middle of the summer.

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text: ACRES OF TEXANS HEAR COL, ROSCOE

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Crowds Storm to Listen to Orator on His Greatest Southern Tour

regions.label: text
regions.text: Houston, Tex. May 14.—Hayling spoken to more than 100,000 people in two weeks, Col. Roscoe Simmons has closed his first tour of Texas for this year. Nobody ever got the reception he got in Texas, and nobody ever advanced any came further than he advanced his. From Paris on the north to Galveston from Waco to Beaumont, this man went on a new journey as prophet. Young white men and women sat at his feet along with members of his own race. His work simmed with others in a story book of human progress.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Would Change Race

regions.label: text
regions.text: Words cannot describe his礼貌, nor the love his people and all people have toward him. He is a man of Henry E. 33, one of the brilliant white men in Texas, wrote in his Mortality in Texas, in word pictures rarely heard, pathos just enough, sparkling humor and native wit, he kept his humor under control, in impassioned appeals he asked of his people that they have patience, in being a white man whom he called friend, he said was that of justice. So well was his appeal to the Northern States, he said to the fong and to America; so touching was he in his description of his people, who have his Race, that one almost at times found himself somewhat modifying the ancient words of King Louis XIV, perverse to me be a Negro.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Sponsor of Dunbar Dies in New York

regions.label: text
regions.text: New York, N. Y, May 14,—William Dean Howells (white), the novelist, died here Pussyfus, May 14, upon his return from France, where he had been stricken with influenza during a recent southern tour, and where he had been struck by a stroke. It was Dr. Howells who first introduced to the world the writings of the late John Laurence Dunbar and encouraged the young poet by his great novelist on several occasions entertained the poet, and when the latter was scrupulously dll in New York City he began a career in publishing. Born in Martin's Ferry, Ohio, March 1, 1837, Dr. Howells served his literary apprenticeship as a compassor, received a degree in paper. When 23 years old he traveled to Boston, Mass, to make the acquaintance of Longfellow, Hawthorne, and William Wordsworth, a boy among musets, who became their intimate friend. At the age of 24 he was appointed by President Abraham Lincoln as United States consul at W. Dr. Howells received degrees from Yale, Harvard, Oxford and Columbia. Dr. Howells had never attended college. He had earned his belief in soci lism.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Knoxville, Tenn., May 14—The A. M. E. Zion conference, in session for eight days, unanimously approving the method of the three churches of Methodism. The quadrennial address was delivered by Wilson George Blackwell, the president of a committee on unity of churches made its report and the address which carried the motion was delivered by Bishop James Husby with reports from general officers. The educational secretary made a splendid report, also the report on the motion, and the report presented that there will be about three bishops elected this year. Rev. Blackwell of Chicago, IL., is being bounced in session another week.

Columbus, Ohio, May 14—Lucius L. McGee, general president of the Alpha Phi Alpha fraternity, was the convocation sponsor at Ohio University and the University of Michigan. The President, Mr. McGee as the speaker for this occasion was the result of an offer of the local chapter to sponsor the fraternity, and the convocation. President Thompson of the university willingly permitted them to present a program before the convocation body on this their tenth anniversary.

regions.label: text

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Girl Slays Self When

regions.label: text
regions.text: Galloway, Ark. May 14—During the absence of the wife who went out to post a letter, Ella Kerr, 16, ended her life by fire a bullet from a revolver into her breast. When the grandmother returned to the house, she found her wife's lifeless body she found the girl's lifeless body she found her hand clutching a revolver. Only one shot had been fired. Motion for the suicides could not be decided.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Sloux City, Iowa, May 14—The New Grob building and Improvement Company filed articles of incorporation in county, recorder of deed, with County Judge, to construct the business will be conducted by our people and will be run for the purpose of handling property and the benefit to the people who are expected to arrive in this city during the summer from such cities as Memphis, Atlanta, Savannah, L.A., and other points south where the prejudice has been at fever heat.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: TWO BOYS SHOT

regions.label: text
regions.text: Mystery surrounds the shooting of Richard Fletcher, age 14, of 2481 Westchester, who was aged 15, of 456 East 24th street, who were fired upon by a white man, at 9 o'clock Tuesday night. About 9 o'clock Tuesday night, the people were found lying soneless on the site, with a man who patrols them but in the vicinity. It is the belief of several people in the neighborhood that the white man opened fire, without warning, as it has been reported that several holdups of fire have been found. The police were unable to obtain a description of the fire, so the hides are probably fetally wounded.

The all school musical concert Monday evening was representative of the various colleges and uni- ties of the A. M. E. church and showed marked talent and musical trailing.

Ald. Louis B. Anderson and the honor

preside over a Republican district con-
vention. The meeting was held at the
oldman's home, where the addernman was elected temporary and permanent chairman of the First congressional district, which is represented in congress by Martin B. Madden
(white). It comprises the First, Sec-
cular and Third Congresses of the city of Chicago. There were 59 delegates present, of which 26 were members of the Race. The convention elected the state central committeeman, Adolph Marxis; nominated the prede-

the right-wing committee to the state
resolution committee to the state

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Brooklyn, N. Y., May 14—As usual, the fourth annual vaudeville and dance of the popular Kismet club lived up to its previous reputation when it held most successful affair at Lobby Hall last month, last one thousand of the younger set were present and danced until the woefull hours of the morning after witnessing the usual vaudeville at the Amphitheater and White, Slither and Groen, Andy Pendleton's Jazzy Four, featuring Milford Smallwood, the wonderful little toon dancer; Dave Watkins from the association's midnight show, Parrell and the Amphitheater, accented by Miss Andrudes and Groen, all the acts were good, each receiving their share of applause. Wm. Brown's society orchestra furnished the music in their usual jazzy style. The Kismet club for the pleasure of their patrons and credit is their for this high class affair.

Brooklynite Drops Dead
Brooklyn, N. Y., May 14—Thophilus Fletcher, a younger brother of W. W. Fletcher, president of the Progressive Improvement association, dropped dead home Monday, May 5, at his home in Montgomery heart disease. Fletcher was a member of the chauffeurs and other organizations. The body was shipped to Elizabeth, Pa., for burial and was acceded a brother of the deceased. A widow and numerous other relatives are left.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Flushing, N. Y., May 14—Because their home was neglected and because they wore without proper guardian-ship, James Lloyd and I. Henry Garner of Wocks avenue, Bayside, were placed in the New York Juvenile asylum. Their father was in court and apparently does a little trucking business, so the court ordered him to pay $2 per week for each child.

Among the candidates for office Dr. F. C. Durden of Mound Bayou, Miss., is looming large for missionary secretary. He is a young man of fine poise and attainments.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Col. Simmons Coming

regions.label: text
regions.text: The general regret of the conference is that Bishop Parks' invitation to Roscoe Conkling Simmons to deliver a speech to the other officer into for acceptance. However, it is likely that he will speak to the great body before the close of the sessions. Col. Simmons has a spot in the men that no other Race man ins, and it would be like depriving a man of his rights to fall to have him speak before this body. The bishops are that "will Roscoe speak" to what time will "that Roscoe speak."

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Candidates for Bishop Active

regions.label: text
regions.text: The election of bishops from all indications is likely to occur Friday, the 14th. W. A. Fountain, president of Morris Brown College, Atlanta, Ga., is very likely to be numbered among the bishops who are as being a man of unusual ability, energy and vigor. He enjoys the unique distinction of being held in high esteem by those who know him as a man of wisdom and humility. Among others who are seeking the bishopric are Rev. C. Runsam, W. T. Vernon, William Docker-Johnson, Bennie C. Caldwell, E. M. Hines, P. P. Peterson, W. W. G. Alexander, and last but by no means least, the matchless financier and church builder, whose name appears to be upon every longo-Dr. Election, and whose election is assured without a doubt.

regions.label: text
regions.text: The foremost candidate for the missionary department, C. M. Tauer, has issued a statement directing the at-terrorism services which could be rendered by a more liberal extension of the work to foreign fields through training courses thereby connecting correspondence and manpower America, West Indies and Abysalia.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: SIDELIGHTS OF CONVENTION

regions.label: text
regions.text:
The Poro college have been the mecca of social events during the conference. It is the home of the great and near-great. Mr. and Mrs. Aaron Malone, the chairperson of the conference, business sagacity and intelligence, the institution has been made famous, a leaving no stone unturned to facilitate the pleasure of their many guests. The institution is a conspicuous example of Malone, who was elected permanent president of the Lay Delegates association, tendered a sumptuous banquet a few nights ago to the visiting doctores of the conference. Speeches were made by a number of representative men, all paying great deference to the builders of the Poro institution, especially Mrs. Aaron Malone, a prominent business woman is unbounded and unpassed by any member of our group.

Mme. Patti Brown Arrives
Mme. Patti Brown, the international song bird, arrived Sunday morning, not to shup, but to present Patti's beauty improvement. W. Mee, Evolven Horton and Mme. W. S. Winston of St. Louis are much in evidence at the Coliseum, acquainting the delegates with the virtues of the group.

Meeting Thursday Night
Dr. Leroy N. Bundy, formerly of East St. Leroy, was presented to the conference Thursday night. Others who took part in the program were Attoroye H., Charles H. Phillips, St. Liesh; C. Linton, Attorney Joseph D. Bibb and A. N. Fields of Chicago; Bishop Joshua H. Jones, Wilberforce; W. T. Vernon, Tennessee. The musical feature of the conference is directed by Prof. Gourg. Gourg of Chicago.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Hon. W. W. Fisher of Evanson is much in evidence at the general convention in is exerting every possible role of Gov. Frank O. Lowden, Mr. Fisher is connected with the office of the attorney general and is one of the right hand men of Hon. Charles G. James, a possible presidential candidate.

The purport of the resolution was, "That there is but one matter how much the people of this earth may vary as to color, hair, language, customs, laws or geographical locations, that the Creator hats indoors upon the earth." The Hillcrest Monograph Co., the Howard Manufacture Co., the National and local Y. W. C. A., the Community Service and Red Cross, New York City Missola Society and many other organizations have promised to assist the community in providing welfare exposition managed by the 16th Infantry. Invitations have been sent to Gen. Pershing, Gen. Wood, Col. Moss, Gen. Lowden of Illinois and others. Gen. Lowden will speak at the forum. It is anticipated that a memorial will be Smith of New York state will deliver the opening address on May 17th. * There will be a church day, woman's day, children's day, American Legion day, soccer day, and Community Service and Urban League days, Gen. Pershing, Theodore Roosevelt and Booker T. Washington days. * Entries of art, sculpture, medal work, fine mechanical work, art, craft, writing and other samples of merit from individuals are being received daily.

Mrs. Alice Jackson's Recital
Mrs. Alice Conrad Jackson announces her musical take to place of her music pupil to take place on Monday evening, May 18, at Harlem Casino, 116th street and Lonox avenue. Upon this occasion Mrs. Jackson will have the assistance of her vocal teacher, Mrs. A. S. Gitterman, the well known Mrs. Gitterman, among Mrs. Gitterman's pupils are among the artists as Mina, M. N. Currie, soprano, co-star for two seasons with John McCormack; Harry Barrhart, leader of U. S. Community chorus, and others. The concert will be followed by dance classes, 8 boxes, seating 8 persons, $2.50 each, at A. C. Jackson's, 129 W, 138th street, Music for dancing furnished by Prof. W, H. Clair.-Advt.

United Homes Club Collation Brooklyn. N. T. Clark co-founded Homes club holds its installation reception Friday evening, April 30, at the residence of Mr. and Mrs. Joseph Askew, 169 Lexington avenue. A fine collation was enjoyed by all present. The officers installed were: President, James secretary, J. H. Smith; treasurer, Joseph Askew; Simmons; Joseph Askew, vice president; J. Rold, assistant secretary, and Wm. Smith, assistant treasurer. The members are Mr. and Mrs. V. Toppins, Mrs. M. Duffy, Mrs. H. Smith, Mrs. J. B along with the guests present were Mr. and Mrs. Massele; Mrs. W. Kemp, Mrs. Blanche Ward, Mr. and Mrs. J. H. Williams and W. H. Smith.-Advt.

Marcolus Dorsey writes in the May Crisis: The average white priest and many of the Catholic bishops readily agree that there should be a priesthood in the gro priesthood—supposedly to conform to American prejudice. What the Negro youth demands and the Raco must have is a priesthood according to the same moral standards as the slim one that provided for all other peoples in the Catholic church.

Suddenly the lift not upward carrying the elevator Salters to the top, crushing the elevator itself and roof of the car. His body was extirpated by the fire department.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
16TH INFANTRY IN BAD WAY
Just how muddy affairs of the 15th infantry are today is apparent in the many realities of officers and the lack of interest of the enlisted men in the regiment. The primary cause is the lack of training. Roberts has fought manfully and hard to have an army erected for the regiment here in Harlem, the problem is now deudocked in the armory board, where it is rumored that nothing will be done to correct it is due to the chaos existing today in the building-trades. The point is made that no new schools are being considered, even. That this should stand in the way of a memorial to the gallantry of the commandant on the gallantry of the world famous 16th Fighters.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Fillmore's Resignation

regions.label: text
regions.text: Whether the resignation of Lieut. Col. Charles W. Fillmore will act to better the regiment is not known just now. Fillmore has always been identified with the 15th. Since his enlistment in the national guard in the mid-1870s he has always been connected with the military in some way. His first commission was with the Ninth battalion of Ohio and his last with the new 15th. His resignation as second lieutenant and pieces he placed in his service officer still lie in the of the guard. He won citations for service during the world war in France and won the croix de guerre on Dec. 6, 1918. Muj. Chislom, who is now acting as executive officer of the regiment, has a fine personality may do something to bring the regiment up from the present depths to which it has gone down.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Success Irritates Some People

regions.label: text
regions.text: For some time New York has been flooded with stock-selling schemes of all nature. Most of the promotors of these schemes try to sell their stock by appealing to Race loyalty: pride, joy. Few, however, ever pay any dividends, and the real reason for investing money. The record of money lost in stock buying is appalling. One concern in New York, however, is actually paying dividends—the Berry & Ross Company. The company pays regular three months' dividends, but an annual has been declared and will be paid the latter part of May. The success of the Berry & Ross people, who first entered into business with the famous Colored dolls, seems to have irritated New York to the end that recently one of them, who, by the way, has floated one of the most gigantic stock selling schemes over put out among our people, referred to the dolls of Berry & Ross as bigger dolls. The attitude of the company never declared any dividends on its stock—never talks dividend, in fact, seems to be one of sour graps.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Round Table Conference

regions.label: text
regions.text: On Tuesday afternoon at 3:30 o'clock in the Y. W. C. A. the Round Table Conference will hold its monthly session. L. Hollingssow Wood, chairman National Urban League, will speak on THE Advantages of a Community Chair." C. L. W. J. W. Lippincott who is connected with the Jewish anthropic Society, will also speak Miss Elizabeth Walton is secretary of the conference.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Tomorrow Comes East

regions.label: text
regions.text: The first copies of "Tomorrow," a new magazine published in Chicago by Nahum Daniel Brusher, editor, and Claudie A. Barnett, director, made their appearance in the East recently. The magazine is excellently made up and features many other titles. It out of our prominent men and writers. It should soon become a factor in molding sentiment for the bottomment of the Race.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: JAMAICA JOTTINGS

regions.label: text
regions.text: Jamaica, N. Y., May 14.—The lecture given by Miss K. M. Johnson last Thursday on the sufferings our men endured in the late war, was well received. It was brought out that the propaganda spread by the southern whites was not well taken by the French and our boys are welcome to attend. It was also the Mother's day at Allon church, and while only one of the speakers booked to speak was present, the services were well attended. The pastor spoke at the morning service and Mrs. P. E. Browster in the evening. The Sunday school banner for collection went to Ms. W. J. Jackson's class and for attendance to the next meeting for the fair will be held Tuesday night and the fair will open Monday night, May 24th. * The Nora P. Taylor Missionary Society will be addressed at 3:20 p. m. on Sunday by Mrs. Rose Edwards. * At the regular meeting of the M. S. Circle at Mrs. Corey Carter's residence, officers were checked in and a new president. The next meeting will be held May 19th at Mrs. E. Marshall's home. * Mrs. Anna Mussle and mother, Huttle Y. Wyatt, formerly of Burlington, Vt., and Edward Fitch are making their home with Pearl Fitch, 79 Cumberland street. * Mrs. Frederika Bay, who has removed from Brooklyn and is now on a public court. * Mrs. Oakley of Ohio has been spending a few weeks at the home of Mr. and Mrs. Childs, 155 Pacific street. * The A. C. Club has renovated the upstairs front room of the parsonage and the dining room.

regions.text: A Poatponed Wedding

regions.label: text
regions.text: Flushing, N. Y., May 14. A marriage of much interest was solemnized last Thursday evening in Macedonia church, having been postponed from April 14, owing to the intended groom being detained in Virginia, due to the railroad strike. The contracting parties were represented by Oxford, NC, and Miss Sarah Randolfo, reception was held at the residence of Mr. and Mrs. Gee. Hicks on Washington street, after which the couple left for their southern home.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Leroy's Now Entertainers

regions.label: text
regions.text: J. W. Mitchell, manager of Leroy's popular cabaret, last week added to his already excellent staff of entertainers two charming singers, Miss Mamie Sharp and Miss Minute Jack. They work and both are dancers of pure ability. Leroy's solves the place of where to go for an evening, and those who wish to dance may do so until morning comes along. The staff of entertainers performing here is larger than that at any similar place in the city.

regions.label: text
regions.text: John J. Cleary (white), 16, of 698 Greenwich street, was arrested last week and sentenced to serve five days in the workhouse by Magistrate Frothman. He was also ordered to roof of his home at several strikebreakers of our Race who were passing through Greenwich street. Magistrate Frothman hold the boy that it was carrying, and after severely lecturing Cleary imposed sentence on him.

York, N. York, M. May 14—Mr. and Mrs. St. Ives, M. 386 Rivera avenue, last Saturday evening. Cards and music were enjoyed during the evening. Among those present were Mr. and Mrs. J. Valdez, Mr. and Mrs. G. Newkins, Mrs. E. Bush of Manhattan, Mrs. E. Messrs. H. Rue, Claud and Hurry Spotsett. Some of the choir members of Mossiah Baptist church led a surprise on Mrs. Daisy Rux last Saturday night. Mrs. Rux received many handsome gifts, and refreshments were enjoyed by the guests. regions.label: text
regions.text: THE CHICAGO DEFENDER

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Many Attend Monster Meeting

Seven hundred people attended the concert of the joint organization movement for the enforcement of the 14th and 15th amendments held at Carnegie Hall last Thursday night, under the auspices of a combination of literary, war, and elite societies. The meeting was held civic society. The event was for the enforcement of the two amendments. Among the speakers who told of the accomplishments of the Race, especially after the abolition of slavery, and of many valorous words in the more recent world war were Rev. R. A. Porter, Mrs. Lawton, Rev. W. H. Hurte, Prof. H. T. Harvey, Hon. Andrew B. Humphrey (white), and Hon. George B. Sims. It was announced from the platform that T. Walker, known as the "Black Spurgeon," of Augusta, Ga., who was born in Boston, spoke, would not be present on account of pressing business in his home. A committee of three, consisting of Counselor A. B. Cozey, Messrs. Handolph and Hinton, were appointed after a motion had been passed to hear a resolution to be sent to every congressional member of the United States and to both the Republican and Democrat national conventions at Chicago and Sun Francisco. Letters were read by Mme. M. Waller French from Mayor E. P. Smith of Chicago, Neb.: Senator Harding of Chicago, Neb.: Senator Harding of Chicago, Neb.: J. Alen of Kansas, all expressing therein the views of the enforcement of the amendments. Among those who took part in the concert were Mme. Elizabeth W. Mayfield, who recited the Battle Hymn of the Republic: little Miss Gr鲁德儿 R. violin solo, accompanied by Miss A. R. piano. The 14th regiment band furnished music for the evening. The audience contributed $302.50 to carry on the good work.

The board of directors consist of the following well known contemner: Claudio Reid, Leroy G. Williams, Frederick Nichols, R. W. Justice, James E. Williams, and Wm. Haley, general manager. The company is incorporated and capitalized at $15,000.

regions.label: text
regions.text: SATURDAY, MAY 15,

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text: On the 25th of May, 1919, MADAM C. J. WALKER, widely known for her unparalleled achievements and her broad philanthropies, passed into The Great Silence.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
BATI
ADAY, MAY 18, 1920

regions.label: text
regions.text:
e 25th of May
KER, widely known for her unparallel
ilanthropies, passed into The Great Si

regions.label: text
regions.text:
IT WAS all so near to us then that we called it death, but today, as we recall her life among us, we say with the poet, "There is no death." A great soul had borne its load cheerfully, had accepted defeats and victories with equal poise, had completed its labors while it was day, and, when the night came, it went to sleep. Those who were near her recall with pleasure that she was cheerful, hopeful, brave to the last, and looked forward with joyful anticipation to the life before her, waiting by the shores, worn, but not tired of life and its changeful struggles. When the time came for embarkation, and the ropes were cast off, her craft now at sea, we would believe that she still looked forward with hopefulness to the new world, to which the unknown voyage should bring her.

Those privileged to witness the throng that paid its last respects to her memory, who listened to the eulogies spoken by those of high attainments in life, white and Colored, cannot forget—so impressive were the many lessons brought home to them. We did not understand, and we almost asked why she went away, just when her influence was becoming a power for the advancement of the Race—just when we needed her most, and it was hard to realize and understand that His ways are not like man's. It was Madam Walker alone who could look up through it all and say, "It's all right."

regions.label: text
regions.text: What can we say of her? To say that she was born in Delta, Louisiana, in 1867, left an orphan at the age of seven, married at fourteen, only to be left a widow soon, with a little daughter to rear and educate by her own earnings as laundress and common laborer, that she started later with a capital of $2 and within twelve brief years accumulated a million dollars, would be, in the words of the artist, to put on "flat colors." The high lights of the canvas will come from the measure of service she rendered humanity; from the way she lived up to the light that she had; from the way she met defeats and victories; from her faith in the Negro Race; from her loyalty to her friends, and her generous disposition toward those less friendly inclined, and at last, from her great faith in God and her willingness, in the midst of every comfort life could offer, to set forth with Him on the last great venture.

regions.label: text
regions.text: When the word went forth that a Negro had passed away, leaving an estate valued at $1,000,000, including a mansion on the Hudson, the world stood aghast. There were those who called it "The last straw of Americanism;" others, the marvel of the twentieth century; others said it was the work of genius, while many referred to her's as the greatest achievement of the Negro Race. A few were left without words. They could not understand it then, and they can scarcely believe it now. Sphinx-like, they have waited for a broader comprehension, for a sixth sense, as it were, to understand how a member of the so-called inferior race, one descended from slave parents, born in that state that offers the meagerest educational opportunities, could establish a colossal business, grapple with the problems of big finance, and build up a mansion for herself in the most favored spot in America. The most conservative of the stronger races were forced to conclude that genius is not limited to any particular race; that it is possible for a member of the Negro Race to overcome the handicaps of centuries in a single generation; that it is impossible for human beings to forecast the personal equation. Some have endeavored to seize upon the secret of Madam Walker's success, but it has seemed more or less elusive. Doubtless she herself would have said it was hard work, intense application, determination not to yield to discouragements, and ambition tempered with Christian ideals. Indeed Madam Walker's career was a marvel—it was more. It was a timely proclamation to Anglo-Saxon civilization, declaring that the way can not be obstructed; a message to the world that the Negro can reach the American standard; it was Destiny's challenge to the closed door of opportunity.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Madam Walker was endowed with initiative, zeal, sympathy, ambition, understanding, and that rarest of all gifts, uncommon common sense. She recognized her weaknesses and her strength, her mistakes and her successes, her possibilities and her human limitations. Well has it been said of her that her heart was broader than any raco, her faith greater than any creed, and her life larger than any circumstance. She was a woman of vision, and her courage was always equal to her tasks.

regions.label: text
regions.text: What was her mission? History bears out the belief that for every great crisis Providence has raised up some one to fill the need. To many it seems that Madam Walker came to give economic vision to a struggling race. Before her time the way to the highest goals seemed so steep and rugged! Colored Americans were not accustomed to let their thoughts "soar into real money." Hence it was not strange that a smile went round when Madam Walker expressed her desire to become a millionaire, in order that she might accomplish the good she desired for her Race. She thought big things, and, as a result, she did big things. What a singular coincidence, or was it a coincidence, that Madam Walker amassed a fortune just at the time when the Racoe was beginning to experience industrial and economic emancipation? At a time when the Race most needed an object lesson in thrift. The lesson is, "Go thou and do likewise." This is not saying, either, that wealth was Madam Walker's chief desire, as an end in itself, but it was her strong desire as a means to an end.

regions.label: text
regions.text: In her ideals Madam Walker was a pioneer. She was privileged to set new standards for business, for Christian giving, for home-making and racial co-operation. According to published statements, Madam Walker was the first Colored woman to give $1,000 for the promotion of Christian work. After making this gift, her appreciation for service increased in proportion to her large income, and throughout her entire career, as a woman of wealth, Madam Walker devoted serious thought to constructive giving. Then it was not her large contributions, that the world knew about, that made her life one of helpfulness, but it was the many little human charities of which the public knew nothing, that distinguished her giving. It was not unusual to find her car parked at the entrance to some alley, while she went among the lowliest, distributing gifts, giving money where it was needed, and all the time giving sunshine and encouragement. It was her custom at Christmas time to remember the poor of her home city with Christmas baskets containing everything necessary for a good Christmas dinner. Often she visited the jails and encouraged the wayward. Where it seemed that the guilt had not been sufficiently established against the accused, she provided

regions.label: text
regions.text: PAGE FIVE

regions.label: text
regions.text: the services of an attorney to see that justive was given. In many instances she provided bonds, without charge, where there was no one to look after the unfortunate ones. As she went about over the country, meeting often with the downhearted and the poor, she gave money for immediate relief in hundreds of instances, and went about her way, leaving no story of her deeds, but only a beautiful memory in the hearts of those she helped. She believed it was worth while to lend money to persons struggling to buy homes for themselves, and in not a few instances she helped others to help themselves. She believed that the talent in the Race should be cultivated, and when young people were without means it is well known to what extent she offered financial encouragement. She was not discouraged, either, when her benefactions were bestowed upon the undeserving, when she judged wrongly, but was just as willing to try it the next time, lest the deserving should go unassisted.

regions.label: text
regions.text: As has been stated above, Madam Walker had great faith in the future. She could look down through the years to the time when benighted Ethiopia should stretch forth her hands to God! Acting in the light of this faith, she set aside a part of her fortune for the establishing of schools of learning on the Dark Continent, and for several years she maintained scholarships in Colored institutions for the education and preparation of young people who had signified their desires to give their lives to work among their people in Africa. She had faith in the ultimate getting together of the Race, and she set a high standard and example in racial co-operation at all times. On the other hand, when giving to charities and worthy causes, her name was always found alongside those of the white race for public causes, even though her own Race was to receive little or nothing of her gifts, for her heart was larger than race.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Madam Walker had a vision of the young people of her Race filling positions commensurate with their worth and training, and to this end she was ambitious to contribute her part. The result is the great system of C. J. Walker agents in every part of the country, and in many foreign lands; the establishment of C. J. Walker beauty parlors, operated by Colored women and cared for in a way that deserves special mention. In her factories are employed a large number of Colored men and women of special educational training and efficiency, performing the same kind of work exactly as members of other races are performing, in places where the same opportunities are not open to Colored people. Her life was a sermon to Americans of Color, setting forth the truth that the Race must establish its own business concerns and employ its own young people if it would measure arms with other races.

Madam Walker had insight into human nature. She studied people. She knew whom she could trust, and her faith in them was unshakable. To those unworthy of her trust she taught the lesson that one must be self-reliant and trustworthy.

regions.label: text
regions.text: What shall be said of her courage? Only a few have shown the remarkable degree of courage exhibited by Madam Walker. When she entered upon her work, that was to bring her a fortune in a few years, her friends thought it an "end-of-the-rainbow" venture, that would end disastrously. Discouragements came thick and fast. As she went from place to place advertising her hair preparations the cold reception was not an unknown happening, but she had made up her mind that "none of these things shall move me," or, a better translation, "none of these things shall keep me from moving." Did she move? The great Tuskeegian was right when he said, "If you keep a man in the ditch, you must stay down there with him," but he spoke only part of the truth. He might have added, "and you must be the better man, for all can't be kept in the ditch." There is a distinct class that is willing to stay down if by so doing it can keep others down. This class is unwilling to make the struggle to the top, and its energies are diverted to holding others down on the same level with themselves. If stumbling blocks and knocks could have curbed Madam Walker's free, brave spirit, Villa Lewaro in Irvington-on-the-Hudson would have been only the shadow of a vanished dream.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Madam Walker struck a new note in human relations when she refused to forget the steps by which she reached the top. Shakespeare was thinking of his own race when he wrote, "But when he once attains the utmost rounds, he looks into the clouds, scorning the base degrees by which he did ascend." Madam Walker never forgot that she was once an orphan, poor and neglected, that she had lived in humble surroundings, that she had worked hard for a living. She never forgot those who rendered her any service, however small, in the days of struggle. She never rose above her friends. Even in her mansion she entertained her humblest friends. She provided the expenses of many who could never have seen her beautiful home otherwise. She was able to adjust herself to conditions and to the last was able to be perfectly comfortable in modest homos; nor were her friends embarrassed in her presence. She believed herself that there is no great or small, so long as any one is doing his best.

A few years ago the strenuous life which Madam Walker loved so much began to tell on her physical condition, and she was told that she must give up her activities, but they had become so much a part of her very life that she could not easily follow this advice. There were friends and admirers who felt that it would have been an easy thing to do, and that her life might have been prolonged for years. They did not know that for her, and others like her, it is far easier to go on than to stop. Given her choice, there was no doubt that she would have preferred making a name for herself in a short time rather than a long life unknown. She preferred the "day of crowded life to an age without a name."

regions.label: text
regions.text:
The only difference in people, after all, lies in the things they believe, and finally Madam Walker believed that she was indeed the captain of her own soul. And, acting in this great faith in the invincible, she went about life in earnest, undaunted, hopeful, and with a great faith. She made no apologies for her existence and looked upon herself as the equal of any. When she found that the lack of educational training was a handicap, she set to work and with studious effort overcame this great obstacle, for to her obstacles were only a challenge to her powers, and as such she met them. As she went about the land delivering public addresses those who heard her recognized continual growth. She mastered the intricacies of business and filled her place uniquely and will forever be an inspiration to those who tremble in face of handicaps and natural hindrances.

How truly has it been said that the subject of this sketch left her mark. The generations coming after her will be the better because of this noble heritage. When the shadows hang low, and when it seems that God has forgotten, the memory of one who has fought and won, and who has lived the simple life of trust, will enhearten and bid the weary and tried to press on, because

"It matters not how straight the gate.

regions.label: text
regions.text: is a 'interior drama, strong in truth, romance and human in ternary, in an music plot, the entail rising on a boy and girl in a modern Garden of Eden, far from civilization. They are the heirs of a wealthy man who has original ideas to no manner in which children should be reared. He figures that if they are brought up in the way that children of the wealthy are raised, that the wealth in which he has accumulated will be foolishly squandered. Therefore he sends them far from civilization, placing them in the care of an aged tutor, who will keep watch over them until they reach maturity. Then they will be taught to live in the world and mind and body. The serpent in the garden is avoided, but upon their return to civilization the serpent lurks at their every step, and we find them engulfed in all sorts of evil, as if they were brought up in it. To tell more of the story would be to rob you of the pleasure it is sure to be yours when this company of players is presenting the play.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Chicagoans had their first opportunity to see the much heralded Ide Anderson in action. This handsome and talented young actress is one of the greatest assets of which the Quality Amusement corporation can boast, and the talent of Anderson gives an impressive display of her line ability. As Evo she is all that could be asked for; she takes full advantage of all the chances and is equally at home in either tense or lighter situations. Miss Anderson is bound to become popular with the crowd given Lawrence Chonault, always a great favorite of the crowd, proud as Adam, handling the part with his usual skill. Chonault is well fitted for characters of this sort, as is Babo Townsend as Seymour Purchall, a heavy character that requires a world of ability for interpretation. Babo is without a doubt one of the most conspicuous performers in the business and can always be relied on. As Old Winters, Sidney Kirkpatrick does some wonderful character work; it is a type that gives him plenty of chances, and he accepts them all. The popular Ingene, Alice Gorgas, as Chirce, has never been seen to battle advantage, and the same can therefore be said of Mrs. Tupper-Bellamy, a man as Mrs. Tupper-Bellamy, a man that seems to have been built to suit that come to have been built. Susie Sutton as Trixie is also well cast and does more than justice to the part. Ivy Hubbard as Mrs. Connyhee does and so does Helen Wilson as Matilde. And Anderson gives a fine portrayal and the cast is completed by A. B. DeConchierre and Harry Plater us the first and second guests.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: THE GRAND

regions.label: text
regions.text: Irvin C. Miller and his Broadway Rustus are still packing them in at the Grand. There is no doubt but what this show is proving the best box office attraction that ever played his house, and it is a fact that the show allows him to enjoy it his enjoying. Emmett Anthony is surely showing that he is no false alarm as a blakface comedian; this young performer is a riot, and working with the elongated Irvin has a million chances and cracks down on all reactions with whom he to hold your funny bone. He has a peculiar style that will not stand to be overlooked, and neither of these artists overplay themselves at any stage. Will Fountaine is as handsome a straight as ever graced the boards the Grand, and aside from his characterization of the voice that is far above the average, Ernie Johnson, the miniature character artist who does the Parson, is another individual who deserves and gets more than passing attention. He gets many a laugh throughout the show, his kisses, his winks, the McCarran Legend Sisters, Ernest Whitman and the entire chorus are all working as smoothly as clockwork, and the fact that another week has been added to the run of this offering testifies better than any orine or mine could to the opinion which Miller and his spindle aggression. If you haven't seen Rustus, get busy; if you have seen him, go again, for

regions.text: THE MONOGRAM

regions.label: text
regions.text: A fine line-up is here for your approval this week. Billy Higgins, known as one of the best comedians of the present time is featured, and he is known for his game. Billy has a line of talk and a number of specially written song numbers that make him an instant favorite. Joe and Lillian Russell are two who are as popular as on their former tour. They added several new features that got a world of mitt. Boykins & Williamson, who have also been seen in the past, are going over good and bad material, and charts, newly formed but efficient, big and completes as good a bill as could be desired. No changes until Monday.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: AGAIN

regions.label: text
regions.text: Again popular demand forces the Grand theater to add another week to the run now in progress by "Broadway Rustus of 1920." This show is breaking all attendance records at this popular house, and there is no denying the fact that if we judge from the image, this is the biggest and best attraction ever seen on the Stroll. Next week is the final one, for sure.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: GEO, DAY'S LETTER

regions.label: text
regions.text: Hello, Tony: Well, guess you thought I was going or gone away. Well, I have left N. York. You know I was Washington working in S. H. Dudley as general manager. I have been here a week and like it very much. The circuit is growing right along and bids fair to be as large as the United white circuit. Mr. Dudley has two theaters here and one in Petersburg, Va.; also working in New York. Mr. Dudley cut, Say, Tony, S. H. Dudley bought a big apartment house not long ago and gave a banquet to christen it April 29, and all of the big guns that were here—oh, my. Get those titles of some or those among those preson, viz.: Wilson Thomas, Correll, Calhoun, Chase editor of the Washington Bee: Finley Wilson, editor of the Washington Eagle; Lawyers Bonl. Gaskins, Phil Coney, Mortimer Harris, Drs. Watkins and Houston. Theater managers and promoters were there also, viz.: Wilson Thomas, Manager of the theater, Chase editor of the theater, Hawthorn and Porator theaters, and Murray brothers, managers Now Dunbar theater. Oh, Tony, but it was some night, and the brain that put forth in addresses and eulogies in book form would be worth a fortune. Hon. Scott and Judge Perrell made women directors, about 4 a., only, Well, no other than the one and only Bort Williams, and then the fun began. He and S. H. embraced each other and started to talk over old times. Strut Payno was there also and made a great speech. Mr. Dudley is making a speech about 4 a., only, Well, n

regions.text: Loyal Hearts is playing the Lincoln theater, 3132 State street, Thursday and Friday of the present week. This is the greatest picture ever made with a stage that has coated it sensation at its overly appearance. Don't fail to lamp it.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: USUAL HIT

regions.label: text
regions.text: The Old Time Darkies' Quartette, known as the Old Standbys, are at the classical Majestic Theater, Chicago, this week, and, as usual, the lads are mopping up. "Our" acts are all going great on the big time. The fellow who roarked that "any old time you see 'em they're the best" sure said a face full. This four, like one-half of one per cent, with rains added, gets stronger with the heat. The business has a quartette stood the acid test for a long number of big time consecutive seasons as has this one. They sure have got 'em.

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text: "HER FIVE-FOOT HIGHNESS"

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: "HER FIVE-FOOT HIGHNESS"

regions.label: text
regions.text:
An opportunity to display her skill as a horsewoman, as the manager of a Texas-cattle ranch and also to look charming in pretty gowns as the heiriness of a big English estate, is given 18th Roberts, the pretty Universal star, in her latest Universal picture, "Her Five-Foot Highness," which comes to her on the holiday Hunter Saturday, as her Roberts established her own an expert rider when she appeared with Frank Mayo in "Lusca." Later in "The Trillers," a society drama, she showed that she was equally at home in a drawing room and that she could wear pretty gowns in a most attractive way, her latest Universal release, "Her Five-Foot Highness," she appears to advantage both on horseback in Texas and as the center of attraction at an exclusive function in English society circles.

Supporting Mias Roberts in her last picture, which was directed by Hany Lennard, is a favorite of favorites, including Virginia Waro, Harold Miller, Stanhope Wheateroff, Kathleen Kirkham, Ogden Crane, Hugh Saxon, Rudolph Christians, Leona Lorrane, Leonard Clapham and Henry Woodward.

Mias Roberts in her Five-Foot Highness "Mias Roberts as a Texas girl is left an English estate by her uncle, the Duke of Wilshire, and establishes her identity only after many complications.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: SOCIAL RUDS

regions.label: text
regions.text:
The following letter which arrived last week will show 'that the Old Time Darkies' Quartet is there like a duck when it comes to functioning—socially. We shoot:  
Hello, Gen. Antonio—We have played two engagements in our home state, dear old Indiana, and they were pleasant ones. I run down to my old home, Indianapolis, and had a very pleasant family with my dear old dad and family. Dad hated this year and he said that my presence seemed to help him some, even more than a doctor's medicine. Had the good fortune of hearing part of the address made by America's greatest orator, Roscoe Conkling Simmons; he is sure himself. We sang at the meeting morning.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Met one of our old friends here, in the person of Adah Banks: it was a great surprise. I'll assure you. She sure looks like a ralloon dollar. She sure looks like a ralloon dollar. She is engaged in concert work. Miss Namile Mao Glover is the accompanist; this latter is one of Evansville's most accomplished indies. She assisted Miss Banks in entertaining the quartet at the enjoyable evening. Will see you week of 10th, when we play the Majestic Theater, Chicago. Be good. Your, Pal.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: SUSIE SUTTON IN

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Like a ray of red sunshine came Susie Sutton, the accomplished actress of the present company of Lafayette Players, on rainy Tuesday afternoon. Miss Sutton, who has the distinction of being the most versatile person in the business, was in a reminiscent mood, as put on one of the finest panning sets that ever took place in this village. Miss Sutton sure looks like a bunch of ready money, and declares that she always looks forward to her trips to Chicago with more than an ordinary amount of jubilation, so to speak.

The Dewey Trio are at Elkhart, Ind, and going great, as usual.

regions.label: text

Philadelphia, Virginia. Lesson in the lead.

regions.label: text
regions.text: THE CHICAGO DEFENDER

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: "PLEASE GET MARRIED"

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Next week at the Avenues theater the Chicago home of Quality Productions, the company of Lafayette Players, which opened their western engagement this week, will be seen in Olive J. Morsose's hilarious bedroom fare "Pilgrimage of the Earth."

This whirlwind honeymoon farce which is funnier than "Baby Mine," more miffful than "Twin Reds" and more laughable than "Fair and Warmer," ran for 30 consecutive weeks last season at the Fulton theater, New York, where it was procured at no small expense by the Quality Amusement Corporation production in the various theaters around its circuit.

Follow the side splitting experiences of an cloaking couple, an frate parent of the male sex, a camouflaged minister about to retire for the night, they make up a burglar, on the honeymoon resort where the bride and groom have planned to spend the first twenty-four hours of their honeymoon. But in the bridal suite of the mountain hotel, just as the honeymooners about to retire for the night, they have had a prohibition wedding, what they have had had has no kick to it; in other words, that their wedding was a filly. For they learn that the minister who officiated in the tying knot was bogus—a more burglar in disguise.

It is bad enough to be alone with a strange young woman on the first night of your honeymoon when you are really and truly married to her, but when you think you are and find you aren't that life's most embarrassing to you, you really want to be entertained, to you really want to real norriment, follow the honeymoon trip with the clooping couple in "Please Get Married."

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: "LOCKED LIPS"

regions.label: text
regions.text:
At the States Theater next Sunday theater-goers of this city will be given an opportunity to see the Japanese Bernhardt, Tsuru Aoki, in her latest photodrama, "Looked Lips."

The success scored by Miss Aoki in her first Universal production, "The Breath of the Gods," has established this Japanese actress as one of the foremost figures on the screen.

Like Lips differs from any other photodrama, Japanese character holds the center of the screen. Its scenes are not laid in Japan. There is no theme nor situation to the story that is foreign to an American audience.

An early opens in a clubroom in an American college house is Stanwood, in a brave over a game of cards shows a man and makes his escape. Fleeing from justice, his wanderings bring him to the island of Hilo. There he breaks into a cottage in search of food. It is the home of Lotus Blossom, a Japanese college native school. She nurses him back to the island and strength and they are eventually married. The man's real nature soon ascents itself and he deserts Lotus Blossom.

His escape from the island, the changing of clothing with a body which had been washed ashore, his to an American girl in San Francisco and Blossom's real romance, which is interpreted when she finds that her husband is married to the woman who had befriended her, are told in a series of interactions that end in a breathless culinary

regions.label: text
regions.text: The production shows the directoral touch of William C. Dowlan, while all players are surrounded by such players as Stanlbom and Magda Lano, Jack Abbey, and others.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: LULU COATES & CO.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Washington, May 14—Hurst & Seamons' big show is playing its second engagement of the season at the glitzey theater and drawing fine views of the week. This aggregation always finds them in this house. It is a burlesque show that is far above the average and it carries many excellent features. The special added attraction is the popular act of Lulu Contes & Crackerjacks. Washington audiences. It is the best out of its kind in the show business today and the sensational work of the group never fails to stop proceedings. It is rumored that this act will travel to New York again next season making the fourth time around, consecutively. Next week, Gayette theater, Pittsburgh, Pa.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: ON MY TRAIL

regions.label: text
regions.text: A letter arrived on Monday from Johnny Wood, the ventrioloquist, who is now a big hit on the Loew time. Johnny was playing Dayton last week and there was a Hebrew performer on the Loew that Johnny that the Old Rul Top Desk Member known in the blz as Louie Levy, so Johnna hauls off and asks us if our name was over Lovy and are we a Yahooga. Yes, Johnny, we are both and Johnny is hitting them behind the Loew in Cleveland, Ohio, this week. He is billed as vanderville's versatile ventrioloquist, which fits him proper.

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text: "DR. JEKYLL AND MR. HYDE"

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Robert Louis Stevenson's powerful story, "Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde," has been translated to the screen with John Barrymore in the leading role and will be shown at the Vendome theater for three days, commencing on Thursday of the present week.

This is declared the most remarkable pictures over filmed, and the performance of Mr. Barrymore, it is claimed, is a worthy companion to that of Richard Mansfield, appeared in the same part in the stage play of Lord Macaulay.

The plot, as is well known, has its central figure a young London physician, Dr. Jekyll, whose tender-heartedness is exceeded only by his all-absorbing interest in his chosen profession. He is an esteemed inocent beautiful girl, Millicent Curew. Her father, Sir George Curew, is a famous man about town. Dr. Jekyll is persuaded by Sir George to accompany him to a low musical hall. From his study of his scientific investigations, he is appointed with a liquid that will separate the good and evil in a man into two distinct bodies. He evolves such a drug, administers it to himself and becomes not only Jekyll, the doctor-shaped, but also Hyde, a misshapen, villian god. Gradually, side of his character overmasters Jekyll, and after attaining dramatic scenes, he succumbs. Only after his death is the dual character of his person revealed.

Northshire man is Mr. Barrymore's leading woman and the picture, which is a Paramount Artery, was directed by John S. Robertson.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: AL WELLS'SHOW

regions.label: text
regions.text: All Wells' Smart Sot, after playing to record breaking crowds at Raleigh, N.C., last week is at Petersburg, Va., the present week. It is the largest Racial show under canvas. It is a great show with an elaborate and creative visual clips and a chorus that makes the natives all along the line sit up and take more than passing notice. Carrying thirty fast and capable workers, it takes to deliver the goods they have to sell. Following acts: Knaney & Reese, Matlock & Matlock, Camp & Camp, Madison & Benbow, Wells & Wells, Pharr & Clayton, Forbes & Burleigh, Adams & Hawkins, Berry & Cushingberry, Jussen & Jussen, Aship, Mae Williams, a composer, Clara Smith, the Blues singer, and a chorus that fits a classy lineup of this sort. In the orchestra are Claud Mantley, piano; Clarence Adams, clarinet; Eddie Carr, trumpon; Clarence Hillill, drums. The entire show sends regards to all friends in and out of the profession.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: EFFUSION

regions.label: text
regions.text:
So you refuse to publish my latest poem, hey?

Well, Tony, I surrender; here's all I have to say:

Glimme back my Springtime Poem, my Old Holl Top Desk Man,
I sent it in you because 'twas all I want.

But if you will forgive me for that last attempt of mine

I vow the one I send you will be better the next time.

Sometimes I write a good one, and other times I don't.

But I always like your censoring, be doggone if I don't.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: THE SCOTTS

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Richard Scott and wife of the Con. T. Kennedy shows spent two days in Chicago last week in a visit to his aunt, Mrs. Annie Debardlebien, of 6540 Grove avenue. Mr. Scott has been with the Con. T. Kennedy shows for five great responsibility with that company. He has absolute charge of the sleeping and dining of this vast aggregation, having the sole management of nine sleeping cars, one private car and the dining car. He has nine assistants and cares for 450 people. His position is unprecedented for a member of the Con., the responsible manager of such a nature that it would be almost impossible to replace him in the person of one individual. For instance, he has charge and operates the motors which generate the electricity for the eleven cars, he lets out contracts for laundry, clothes pressing, etc.; he collects all monies for borths, meals, etc., pays out all primitives for the living discharging of all help whilst his wife, Mrs. Scott, does the catering for the private car, in which travel the owner of the shows and his family. The Con. T. Kennedy shows appeared all of the past week at Elgin, and proceeds from that place to Freeport. Ill. Mr. Scott's season with this company is for thirty hours during the year; finance the time alone in vaudeville as the partner of Prof. Means in the Prince of India Company.

Rucker & Winfred are at the Pantagene Theater, Vancouver, B.C. of Jeff & Rollinson are doing fine on the Fort. Rosa Valyda is at the Pantagene Theater, Butte, Mont.

regions.label: text
regions.text: VIEW

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: "THE ADVENTURER"

regions.label: text
regions.text: William Furnum, well-known mime picture star of Fox Film corporation, who opens at the States Theater on Thursday and Friday, May 20 and 21, in "The Adventurer," a story written by 12, Ines Sheldon, beloves that one of the most valuable a sound and a strong body is fencing. The great actor has been noted for year for the excellence of his swordsmanship, and in "The Adventurer" he proves that only years of training in this appreciated art has enabled him to take his place in the front rank of the swordmen of the world. Furnum is always in training. His photography demand that he keep his body in perfect condition at all times, and he never falls to spend an hour a day in physical exercise. Fifteen minutes of this time he allows for fencing—which demands quickness of eye and great agility. Even before he left the stage for the screen he had mastered the art, and was noted for his ability with the rapiper, broadsword. "There is not a muscle that is not exercised in fencing," said the popular star. "It is not necessary to have a broadsword or a rapiper or a foll. a broomstick will do. Women and men can keep themselves in perfect physical condition by 10 minutes a day with two sticks." "Sweet Adellino' is being sung with focus by Billy 12. Jones, the popular singer.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Dear Pal, Tony: No, Ragtime Billy Tucker hasn't dropped completely out of existence, but is still in the "Big Apple." Los Angeles. Now, right here, Tony, before I go any further, I want to tell the world what I am. Kaneako that a few weeks ago, when I told the Old Roll Top Desk Man that I could place entertainers and musicians in sunny California, I received a "zillion" letters from all over the United States, including Georgia; Wil Maston was in town yesterday, no matter what it is, all you have, do is to whisper to the Old Roll Top Desk Man and hall put you Juke, I'll say, Green & Pugh were a scream at the Pantages last week; some boys, Wil Maston was in town yesterday and the rest of the staff, Brooks & George hold honors at the Orpheum Theater next, week. regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: REAL NOTICE

regions.label: text
regions.text:
"The Commercial Appeal of Memphis, Penn. is no doubt the biggest and most newspaper in the South. Ned Courtington wrote in one of his daily, surely spread himself when typed the following review of a recent concert given in the Tennessee metropolis. We shoot, and judge for you."

"From Beaulock street to Broadway several years ago went Handy with his band, with his vision that the Negro race has and will have a distinctive form of owning, at one day instead being ignored, less of a comic light Negro melody will echo in opera; will be incorporated in the great American composite musical, just as folk songs of New York, and received playback in the house at Church's auditorium last night. More than 1,000 people were turned away and standing room was premium. There was a liberal sprinkling with the appeal who attracted the real merit of the performance, enjoyed over note.

"Handy has caught the spirit of the folk song of the Negro from the fields, and received playback in the mills. Handy has been a worker for still is a worker—a worker for the uplift of his Racoe and the creation of music that has an appeal to the car. "Vory one who loves melody that comes from the worker who gets joy out of his work."

"From the midday, Beaulock Street to Broadway, to the finale, when the orchestra stood and played the 'Memoirs' and which made Handy, there was melody, there was real musical proficiency."

regions.label: text
regions.text:
"The climax of the performance was the overture, Aunt Maga's Childrine; to catch some of the Negro melody and give it a blameless of classic setting. It opened well. Real orchestral skill was demonstrated in the open-ended and the sequence and antiphonal and composition. "It was a demonstration of the beginning of the work of weaving a theme about a cycle of folk songs, or other echoes of folk songs, and the singing of the Negro manmy to her babe.

"The Yellow Dog Blues" probably written over the blue prospect a Mississippi Negro night face when catcher Sammy train out of Clarkedale, perhaps to the successful of Handy's recent compositions, brought the heaviest applause. Had the "Memphis Blues" had the same effect of the program a riot probably would have been.

"There was a liberal outpouring of Colorod society of Memphis to welcome Handy back again and bid him Gospeed back on his path, already stricken."

"There are five Memphis Negroes with Handy and, including the trap drummer, Jasper Taylor, they are all outfists. H. H. Pace, Handy's business partner, H. H. Pace, was recognized as a leader in New York. Lee Concert showed Memphis the reason."

regions.label: text
regions.text: A second concert will be given this afternoon the Grand Theater on Beale avenue."

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: SOME NOTICE

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Here is a little write-up that appeared in a recent issue of the Memphis (Tenn.) Press, the biggest and best paper published by white folks in the area.

"Handy's famous band, which has done about as much as anything else to put Memphis on the map, may return to this city about May 16 to give a concert for white people. B. M. Roddy, cashier of the Solvent Savings Bank, today is trying to make such arrangements. 1,000 Negroes were turned away from Church Park last night, where Handy gave a concert for members of his Race. There was a huge demonstration when the composer of 'The Memphis Blues' led his band in that famous piece.

This afternoon a concert will be given at the Grand Theater, on Peavey avenue, for the band to perform for a Negro dance at Church Park and leave immediately thereafter for Little Rock, the next stop on its present tour.

"Handy, a Memphis Negro, is the originator of a dozen different 'blues.' A few years ago he moved his headache across the street avenue to a new way. H. H. Pace, assistant cashier of the Solvent Bank, is his business partner."

regions.text: THE CHICAGO DEFENDER

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: THE TRIP

regions.label: text
regions.text:
at Harlem and the Harlemites
Nover in the history of the big Pennsylvania passenger pot and prize train in the city — the Broadway Limited—cause less excitement than did the arrival of the Old Roll Top Desk Man on his recent play and prize train in the city and not a gang cheered. Even the Red Caps went to sleep on the boy from in front of the Stock Yards, and he fought his weary way through the three rows in his hand and a Drs. Prescription in the other, he hummed that beautiful though sympathetic time. "What Was I Before He Was What He Is To Today?"

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Clough Apartments

regions.label: text
regions.text: But it was a different story when the traveler struck Harlem. There, in Inez Clough-Hughes, that awaited handsome Inez Clough-Hughes as handsome—so to speak—husband, "Petie" Hogan, the pride of 75 avenue and the Major Domo of the Goldgrabb and the Male Male of the couple, sure turned on a mean man, the wanderer and showed both in the abstract and concrete that the high cost of living doesn't amount to much with them.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Open Car

regions.label: text
regions.text: After bumping off a prodigious amount of provender, Old Roll Top made toward Lenox avenue, where the congregate the thickest. Comes along Baroucha (that's what Lawrence calls it), and nothing must do but I must ride. It was a fine arrangement. After boo, I minutes Lawrence said, "I have to tell you what to do?" I will settle and we will get some cold tea." He whispered in the chauffeur's car and then straightened up, "Wot-tell you talkin' about?" yelled Lawrence, and on sunrise we have to do better than that, and we fight, so I guess Larry "lid it down," which goes to show that gaso in Harlem is worth more than bottled in bond or that chauffeurs see double on Sunrise so they also robe with us the final half block in the bandit judged Chenault's bank roll by the length of Muse's coat-tails.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Charley Thorne

regions.label: text
regions.text: Any one present ever hear of Charley Thorpe? Charley Thorpe who chased the Germans from one end of Belgium to the other with nothing but his hat, his sword, his rifle, his us after Chenault had paid his bill and had bid us good-by. If ever there was a place where a traveler is made welcome, it is at Thorpe's. He has 124 war pictures on the wall and two diaries which "between us or each picture." by this one. The Roll Top Desk Man started seeing double, so he closed shop for the night and heat it back to the Clough-Hogan apartment, where he wan the guest of the third part week, being the party of the third part week. Champion Organ Grinder of Boston, and Pete himself, who became a grass widower on Monday on account of the departure of his better—and bigger—half, for washing, where the Lafayette clipal pillar, held forth for the week, presenting that beautiful play entitled, "Will He Hush What It Was Left, or Buy Ice and Saves It for Me."

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: The Gang

regions.label: text
regions.text: Can't overlook the gang. There was Alex Rogers, Jim Burris, Eldor Forrest, Lester Walton, Bright Eyes, all the Afafayte Players, all the This and Harry Fidder, Jim Stevens, all the gang at the C. V. B. A., where I promised to go and didn't get there; Baw Peyton, Black Carl, the mobs at the C. V. B. A. and Green, Frank Young, the best newspaper man in the cast—and he ain't doing it—Sandy Burns and his gang, Chinese Walker, Yukle Johnson and so many others that I hope they won't be around for not remembering them all.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Coming Back

regions.label: text
regions.text: Ever see a bunch of mourners heading home after the "dust to dust" argument? That's the way it seemed the final morning when Gray and Horan and I started for the station. The Old Roll Top Bank Roll looked like there had been a circus abroad in the United States had done a Salome on it; it was so flat that I mistook it for a cigarette paper while standing in the subwalking for a train, and right here, without fear of sane discussion discussion, do I declare that a trip to New York is zero in moderate priced entertainment. But, thank the Lord, we arrived safely onto an oasis and a satchal with a satchal in each hand and a bottle of citrate of magnolia in the other.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: WILL GAUZE

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Will Gauze, the famous impersonator, who has just completed a long tour of Canada, was a caller at the Old Roll Top Desk on Tuesday, when he looked up to kopecks and fixed the Roll Top Man's jaw out with a couple of fine clear Havanes. He left for a three weeks' visit to Windsor, Canada, his home town. It is understood that he was on the canoe during his stay there, as 'tweow. You know what I mean.

Seymour & Feamonte are at the Windsor & Push ore at the Pantages Theater, Oden, Utah.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: ANDREW BISHOP

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Possibly the most popular male member of the Lafayette Players is the subject of this short match, cherished Andrew Hawkins Bishop. Bishop, who is affectionately designated by his host of friends, was born at Mt. Kisco, N. Y., in 1803. He is a son of Rev. Hutchens Bishop, rector of St. Philip's P. E. church of New York city, and gained his first experience in the show

Andrew S. Bishop

regions.label: text
regions.text: business as a member of Alex Rogers and Henry Creamer's "Old Man's Boy," playing the title role with more than ordinary success. This aggregation of talent was initiated at the end of the season, Bishop invested a few million kopecks in the automobile and garage business. The odor of gasoline palled upon Bish, so late in 1916 he cranked up and joined the Anita Bush Stock Company, which successfully engaged at both the Lincoln and Ford theaters in Harlem. He was also a member of the Mrs. Hopgood's Colored Players, which held the stage at the Garrick theater on Broadway for several weeks. After severing connections with the Ford-colored Stock" he signed with the Lafayette Players, being given small parts. Under the tutorship of Edgar Forrest, the celebrated promoter and director, Bishop developed and advanced until today he stands out as an actor of

regions.label: text
regions.text: Andrew Bishop is, above all things, a "man's man." He stands something like 6 feet in his silk hose and is built along the lines of Paul Jones, who recently won the Kentucky Derby. He stood on the prize ring instead of the stage for a livelihood he would have either made a champion or a mighty fine trial horse. He is what might be called a real specimen of the "mathesus idol" and it is an ordinary thing to hear the his work calls for an eye on the making. He gets more notes at every performance than Wilson sent to Europe before the late peacock conference and hundreds of dollars are spent every season, by the different theaters at which he plays in replacing the glasses on his wrist. He sees by photos from the dust. He wears no jewelry or fashy clothes and among the other splendid facts that go to give him an enviable record is the one that he never has his finger mails manneled in a barber shop window. He wears a wide-brimmed hat ever turned down an invitation to crook his elbow it has never leaked out, all of which goes to show that he is a real fella after our own heart.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: REAL VISITOR

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Oliver Banks, the popular proprietor of the famous Hotel Bilmore, 306 st. Antoine street, Detroit, Mich., was in the city this week. He came on business of great importance and was a guest at the Vincennes Hotel. Oliver is an old Chicago boy and has made many rimoeleons in the Ford town. He was all lit up like a church at night and appeared as if the finger of prosperity had rested heavily upon him. He returned late in the week.

regions.label: text
regions.text: PAGE EIGHT

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text: ALL OF TEXAS HEARS SIMMONS

regions.label: text
regions.text: Houston, Tex., May 14.—Closing a tour of three weeks in Texas, Regison Simmons left the state for North. He spoke to students at 10,000 people, in many places to audiences equally divided as to race; never to any except a mixed audience.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Flaya Jim Crow Car

regions.label: text
regions.text: On this trip the spokesman for the common people jumped on the Jim Crow car, saying to the white people that the Jim Crow car had locked behind its doors the honor of a country. "Let me out," he said, "and honor will walk out with me." Col. Simmons spoke under arrangements made by William McDonald and James B. Grissby, big men among the real big men of Texas. No place on his tour, except the Houston Auditorium, was big enough to accommodate the crowds that wanted to listen to Col. Simmons "lay out" the illly whites. "To say that he advanced the cause is hardly saying anything, for Col. Simmons is alone in his work, both in the extent and character of it.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Simmons Praised

regions.label: text
regions.text:
No such compliment was ever paid a member of the Race as that paid Col. Simmons by the pen of the brilliant Henry Ellis, editor of the Dentson, Tex., Morning Herald, who, after hearing Col. Simmons, wrote in his paper these words:

"In word-pictures rarely heard, with pathos just enough, sparkling humor and native wit, he kept his audience as long as he wished. So well was his appeal to the Negro put that they remain true to the flag, and to America; so touching was he in his description of the ideals to which he would have lived. He kept himself somewhat modifying the ancient words of King Agrippa, 'Almost thou persuadist me to be a Nogro.'"

In almost every city visited, Col. Simmons was welcomed by the mayor, and in several towns he was asked to include white high schools in his rounds.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Heaviest Woman Dies

regions.label: text
regions.text: Lancaster, Pa., May 14--Mrs. Lizzie Clark, aged 54, of 419 North street, who died here last week from dropsy, was privately buried from her home. She weighed 500 pounds at the time of her death. Despite her reputation of being the heaviest woman in the state, and the difficulty she had in getting from place to place as a result of her ailment, which caused her abnormal weight, she continued her hercolent work among her people.

regions.label: text
regions.text: The body was buried in a specially built casket twice the size of the average coffin.

Boston, Mass, May 14.—A bill to
make such persons as habitually mis-
spend their time liable to punishment
under the idle and disorderly act was
filed in the house on petition of Police
Commissioner Edwin U. Curtis of
this city. The bill would make such
persons subject to imprisonment for
six months or by a fine of not more
than $200.

regions.text: JERSEY CITY NOTES

regions.label: text
regions.text: Jersey City, N. J., May 14—A mistake was made in reporting the death of Rev. R. D. Wynn. * The matron's May party, under the auspices of the I. B. P. O. Flicks, last Friday night, was a grand success. * Mrs. Selma Branham and Mrs. Ellington Brown were directors of the affair. * Mrs. M. Tibbs and daughter Wilder will leave soon for Oakland, Cal. * Thomas Cabinas will make Washington, D. C., his future home and will be located at 1744 K street. * The City Federation of Women's Clubs meets regularly the first and third Wednesday night in each month at the X. W. C. A. They are making preparations for the annual meeting July 9. * The Community Center is being visited by many citizens and is the place for recreation and pleasure. * Memorial celebration of the Booker T. Washington post 209, American Legion, will be held at Dickson high school Sunday afternoon, May 30. A splendid program has been arranged. * B. Clon Jones, commander.

BY CAPTAIN JAMES H. SMITH
The American Giants and the Chicago Giants of Chicago hooked up in their annual scrap last Sunday at Schorling Park and, as always, interest was at fever heat. The largest crowd of this season turned out and the Giants was continuous throughout the game, pretty good team together this year and with a little more team work should make good around the circuit. Walter Ball, the old reliable, was wild as a March bare and put his team at a great disadvantage, right after they never recovered, especially while Dave Brown was on the rubber, they simply could not see his offerings. When Tom Williams ascended the mound they seemed to take a liking to his cowper and started to pound them around the lot, but were too far behind to catch up.
